claim: Currier A and Currier B employ two structurally distinct cipher alphabets encoding the same underlying Latin plaintext: the two sub-corpora should exhibit near-identical word-length distributions and Zipf exponents but divergent bigram transition matrices, consistent with two key tables applied to the same plaintext.
evidence_for: Currier A/B split is well-attested in scribal-hand analysis
High-confidence belief (0.501-0.677) that same underlying language is encoded differently across A and B
If plaintext is shared, word-length statistics should converge across A and B even if glyph distributions differ
evidence_against: Belief 0.208 supports two different plaintext languages across A/B
Vocabulary overlap between A and B corpora may be too low to confirm shared plaintext without decipherment
test: Compute word-length frequency distributions, Zipf exponents, and bigram transition matrices separately for Currier A (11,022 words) and Currier B (23,766 words); test whether word-length distributions are statistically indistinguishable (KS test) while bigram matrices differ significantly (chi-squared or Jensen-Shannon divergence).
test_metric: KS statistic on word-length distributions; Jensen-Shannon divergence between A and B bigram transition matrices; Zipf exponent difference
test_threshold: Confirmation: KS p-value > 0.05 (indistinguishable word-length distributions) AND JS divergence > 0.15 (distinct bigram structure). Denial: word-length distributions also differ significantly (KS p < 0.01), suggesting distinct underlying languages.

tests_run.test: compute word-length frequency distributions, zipf exponents, and bigram transition matrices separate
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: This test can distinguish 'same plaintext, two cipher tables' from 'two different plaintexts' without requiring decipherment, directly resolving a high-uncertainty split in current beliefs.
